title: Daily Market Digest: Wall Street Caps Fifth Straight Losing Week Amid Geopolitical Turmoil
subtitle: Market insights for March 28, 2026

items.headline: Wall Street Ends Fifth Straight Losing Week as Geopolitical Tensions and Inflation Fears Drive Sell-Off
items.summary: Major U.S. stock indices concluded their fifth consecutive week of declines on Friday, marking the longest losing streak in nearly four years. Heightened geopolitical instability, particularly related to the Iran conflict, propelled oil prices above $100 per barrel, exacerbating inflation concerns and fostering a broad 'risk-off' sentiment among investors. Both the Dow Jones Industrial Average and the Nasdaq Composite have officially entered 'correction' territory, having fallen more than 10% from their recent peak levels. Consumer sentiment has simultaneously deteriorated, slipping to multi-month lows. Not financial advice.

items.headline: Netflix Rises on Price Hikes
items.summary: Netflix saw a slight gain today following its announcement of service price increases in the United States.

items.headline: Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings Falls
items.summary: Consumer discretionary stocks were hit hard by rising fuel costs and recession fears, with Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings experiencing a significant drop.

items.headline: Amazon Among Big Tech Declines
items.summary: Big Tech stocks weighed heavily on the market, with Amazon shares falling amidst a broader tech-sector underperformance.

items.headline: Meta Platforms Experiences Dip
items.summary: Meta Platforms saw a notable decline as tech stocks continued to underperform, partly due to concerns over multiple compression and regulatory risks.

items.headline: Starbucks Shares Sink
items.summary: As consumer spending faces pressure from rising gasoline prices, non-essential retail companies like Starbucks saw their shares sink.

items.headline: Chipotle Mexican Grill Drops
items.summary: Chipotle Mexican Grill was among the consumer discretionary stocks to fall sharply as concerns over consumer spending habits grew.

items.headline: Energy and Defense Sectors Lead, Consumer Discretionary and Tech Lag
items.summary: The energy sector continued its strong performance, rising 18% month-to-date as oil prices surged above $100 per barrel amidst the Iran conflict. Defense stocks also saw significant gains, with companies like RTX and Lockheed Martin up over 19% month-to-date. Conversely, the consumer discretionary sector plummeted 12% in March, impacted by fears of rising fuel costs and a potential recession. The tech-heavy Nasdaq underperformed due to 'multiple compression' and regulatory concerns, with the broader Financials, Technology, Communications, and Health Care sectors also lagging the S&P 500 year-to-date.

items.headline: Fed Discusses Balance Sheet Reduction; Economic Data Shows Slowdown
items.summary: Federal Reserve Board Governor Stephen Miran addressed the prospects of shrinking the Fed's balance sheet, suggesting a gradual reduction of $1-$2 trillion to avoid market disruption. Economic data released today indicated a slowdown in U.S. business activity, with the flash Composite PMI declining to an 11-month low of 51.4 in March. Additionally, consumer sentiment has fallen to 56.4, below the 60-point threshold historically associated with recessionary consumer behavior, while inflation expectations have jumped to their highest level in a year.

items.headline: Mixed Q4 2025 and Q3 2026 Earnings Season Continues
items.summary: Cintas ($CTAS) and Paychex ($PAYX) delivered strong fiscal Q3 results, beating analyst estimates for both EPS and revenue and showing consistent year-over-year growth. Carnival Corporation ($CCL) also exceeded Q1 expectations, though management subsequently lowered its FY2026 adjusted EBITDA and EPS forecasts, impacting investor sentiment. Meanwhile, Talon Metals ($TLO) and TeraGo ($TGO) announced their Q4 2025 earnings today. Braskem S.A. ($BAK) reported a Q4 2025 EPS miss, along with a revenue decline.

items.headline: Iran Conflict and Oil Prices Erase Trillions in Market Value
items.summary: The escalating conflict with Iran has been a dominant macro trend, driving significant market volatility. Estimates suggest that approximately $4 trillion to $5 trillion in market value has been wiped out over the last 28 days due to the persistent decline, as the conflict fuels surging oil prices and renewed inflation fears. The S&P 500's price-to-earnings (P/E) multiple has consequently dropped from 21x to 19x over the past month. The uncertainty surrounding energy prices and geopolitical developments remains a critical factor for market direction.
